severityScales.name: INCAT Disability Scale
severityScales.description: Overall disability sum score from 0 (no disability) to 10 (death due to GBS/CIDP); assesses arm disability (0-5) and leg disability (0-5)

severityScales.name: Modified Rankin Scale (mRS)
severityScales.description: 0 (no symptoms) to 6 (dead); adapted for polyneuropathy disability

severityScales.name: MRC Sum Score
severityScales.description: Sum of muscle strength (0-5) in 6 bilateral muscle groups; max 60; assesses shoulder abductors, elbow flexors, wrist extensors, hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors

severityScales.name: Overall Neuropathy Limitations Scale (ONLS)
severityScales.description: Validated CIDP scale; arm score 0-5, leg score 0-7, total 0-12

diseaseCoursePatterns: Chronic progressive: steady worsening over months to years
Relapsing-remitting: episodes of worsening separated by recovery (30-50%)
Monophasic: single episode with subsequent improvement
Chronic monophasic: slowly progressive then plateaus

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness developing over at least 8 weeks, sensory ataxia, areflexia
motorSymptoms: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness affecting both upper and lower limbs
Proximal weakness: difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chair, lifting arms overhead
Distal weakness: foot drop, hand weakness, difficulty with fine motor tasks
Tripping due to foot drop is a common early symptom
Waddling gait from proximal leg weakness
Facial weakness: mild bilateral facial weakness in some patients
sensorySymptoms: Numbness and paresthesias in symmetric stocking-glove distribution
Sensory ataxia: unsteady gait, worse in dark, Romberg positive
Lhermitte-like phenomenon due to proprioceptive tract involvement
gaitDisturbance: Sensory ataxic gait: wide-based, unsteady, worse without visual input
Steppage gait: high-stepping due to foot drop
Waddling gait: from proximal hip girdle weakness
autonomicSymptoms: Mild autonomic dysfunction may occur but less prominent than in GBS
Postural hypotension, urinary retention, constipation, erectile dysfunction
naturalHistory: Progression over 8 weeks to 6 months (minimum 8 weeks to distinguish from GBS)
Spontaneous improvement occurs but most require immunotherapy
Without treatment, 60% become non-ambulatory at 10 years

motorExam: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness in upper and lower extremities
Proximal: hip flexors, quadriceps, shoulder abductors, elbow flexors
Distal: ankle dorsiflexors (foot drop), intrinsic hand muscles
Diffuse areflexia or hyporeflexia: most consistent sign
sensoryExam: Stocking-glove distribution sensory loss: distal greater than proximal
Vibration sense impaired earliest, often absent at ankles and knees
Proprioception impaired leading to sensory ataxia (positive Romberg)
Light touch and pinprick reduced distally
cranialNerveExam: Facial weakness (CN VII): bilateral, mild to moderate
Bulbar weakness: dysarthria, dysphagia (less common than in GBS)
functionalAssessment: Romberg test: positive with eyes closed
Rising from chair without arms demonstrates proximal weakness

riskFactors: Preceding infection or vaccination: 15-30% report preceding infection within 6 weeks
Autoimmune disorders: diabetes mellitus, SLE, IBD, HIV, hepatitis B/C, thyroiditis, MGUS
Monoclonal gammopathy: IgG/IgA MGUS with typical CIDP; IgM with DADS and anti-MAG neuropathy
Pregnancy: may trigger onset or postpartum relapses
HIV infection: CIDP can occur in early HIV
Lymphoma and malignancies: paraneoplastic CIDP-like syndrome

subtypes.name: Typical CIDP
subtypes.description: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness, sensory ataxia, areflexia, progressive >8 weeks
subtypes.details: Meets EFNS/PNS 2021 criteria; responds to standard immunotherapy; most common subtype

subtypes.name: Distal acquired demyelinating symmetric neuropathy (DADS)
subtypes.description: Distal predominant symmetric sensory loss and weakness; length-dependent pattern
subtypes.details: Associated with IgM MGUS and anti-MAG antibodies; less responsive to standard immunotherapy

subtypes.name: Multifocal acquired demyelinating sensory and motor neuropathy (MADSAM/Lewis-Sumner)
subtypes.description: Asymmetric multifocal weakness in individual nerve distributions
subtypes.details: Upper limb predominance; conduction block; responds to IVIG better than steroids

subtypes.name: Focal CIDP
subtypes.description: Demyelinating neuropathy confined to one limb or region
subtypes.details: Rare; brachial or lumbosacral plexus; must exclude structural causes

subtypes.name: Sensory-predominant CIDP
subtypes.description: Predominant sensory symptoms with minimal motor involvement
subtypes.details: Sensory ataxia, paresthesias; preserved strength; challenging to diagnose

subtypes.name: CIDP with diabetes mellitus
subtypes.description: CIDP in diabetics, difficult to distinguish from diabetic neuropathy
subtypes.details: Proximal weakness, sensory ataxia, areflexia, elevated CSF protein, immunotherapy response favor CIDP

subtypes.name: Autoimmune nodopathy
subtypes.description: Antibodies against nodal proteins (neurofascin-155, contactin-1, Caspr1)
subtypes.details: Aggressive course, tremor, sensory ataxia, poor IVIG response, responds to rituximab

mimics: Guillain-Barre syndrome: acute onset, progression <4 weeks, Campylobacter jejuni, severe dysautonomia
Diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexopathy: acute severe pain, asymmetric proximal leg weakness
Chronic idiopathic axonal polyneuropathy: slowly progressive, purely axonal NCS, no immunotherapy response
Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN): pure motor asymmetric, anti-GM1 antibodies, conduction block, IVIG responsive but not steroids
Primary systemic amyloidosis: autonomic involvement, nephropathy, cardiomyopathy, Congo red positive
POEMS syndrome: monoclonal gammopathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, skin changes, sclerotic bone lesions, elevated VEGF
Charcot-Marie-Tooth type 1: slow progression from childhood, pes cavus, hammer toes, family history, no immunotherapy response
Motor neuron disease (ALS): pure motor, UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski), bulbar onset
Anti-MAG neuropathy: IgM MGUS, distal predominant, tremor, ataxia, anti-MAG antibodies
Paraneoplastic neuropathy: subacute onset, asymmetric, onconeural antibodies, underlying malignancy
Vasculitic neuropathy: mononeuritis multiplex, acute to subacute pain, systemic vasculitis features

bedside: Neurologic examination: full motor, sensory, reflex, coordination, and gait assessment
MRC sum score for muscle strength quantification
INCAT disability scale for functional impairment
9-hole peg test for upper extremity function
laboratory: CSF analysis: cytoalbuminologic dissociation with elevated protein (50-200 mg/dL) and normal WBC <10; cell count >10 suggests HIV, Lyme, lymphoma, sarcoidosis
Serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation: rule out MGUS
Serum free light chains: sensitive for monoclonal gammopathy
Anti-MAG antibodies: if IgM MGUS with distal phenotype
Anti-GM1, anti-GD1a, anti-GQ1b: if MMN suspected
Anti-neurofascin-155, anti-contactin-1, anti-Caspr1: for autoimmune nodopathy
CBC, ESR, CRP: baseline inflammatory markers
Metabolic panel: glucose, HbA1c, TSH, vitamin B12, folate
HIV, hepatitis B/C, Lyme serologies: infectious causes
ANA, ENA, ANCA, RF: autoimmune screening
Onconeural antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-CV2, anti-amphiphysin): if paraneoplastic
VEGF level: if POEMS syndrome suspected

imaging.details: Must demonstrate demyelinating features per EFNS/PNS criteria
demyelinatingCriteria: Motor conduction velocity <80% of LLN (or <70% if distal CMAP <1 mV)
Distal motor latency >125% of ULN (or >150% if distal CMAP <1 mV)
Conduction block: >30% drop in proximal vs distal CMAP amplitude
Temporal dispersion: >30% increase in proximal vs distal CMAP duration
F-wave latency >120% of ULN (or >150% if distal CMAP <1 mV)
imaging.findings: At least 1 demyelinating criterion in 2+ nerves for definite CIDP; study 4+ motor nerves, 3+ sensory nerves, and F-waves

imaging.details: Supports diagnosis if NCS inconclusive; prolonged N13-N20 interpeak latency
imaging.findings: Prolonged central and peripheral conduction times

imaging.modality: MRI brachial and lumbosacral plexus with contrast
imaging.details: Useful when NCS inconclusive or MADSAM suspected
imaging.findings: Hypertrophy and gadolinium enhancement of nerve roots and plexus; T2 hyperintensity

imaging.details: Exclude structural causes; may show thickened enhancing nerve roots
imaging.findings: Hypertrophic nerve roots, cauda equina enhancement mimicking leptomeningeal disease
Increased cross-sectional area of nerves and roots; enlargement more pronounced in CIDP than axonal neuropathies

imaging.indications: Atypical presentations, suspected vasculitis, amyloidosis, or sarcoidosis
imaging.findings: Demyelination, remyelination (onion bulbs), endometrial edema, macrophage-mediated demyelination; lymphocytic infiltration
imaging.note: Invasive; used when diagnosis uncertain or specific alternative suspected

management.protocolReference: EFNS/PNS Guideline on management of CIDP (Joint Task Force, 2021)

firstLine.dose: 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days as loading; maintenance 1 g/kg every 2-4 weeks or 0.4 g/kg weekly
firstLine.mechanism: Modulates Fc receptors, neutralizes autoantibodies, regulates complement, modulates cytokines
firstLine.monitoring: CBC, renal function, liver enzymes; IgA level before first dose (risk of anaphylaxis in IgA deficiency)
firstLine.adverseEffects: Headache, fever, myalgia, nausea, volume overload, thrombosis risk, aseptic meningitis, renal failure
firstLine.evidence: Response rate 60-80%; onset within days to weeks; effective for both maintenance and relapses

firstLine.dose: Prednisone 1 mg/kg daily (max 80 mg) for 4-8 weeks, then slow taper over 6-12 months
firstLine.alternative: Dexamethasone 40 mg daily for 4 days monthly (pulse therapy); methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV weekly
firstLine.mechanism: Anti-inflammatory, reduces T-cell activation, inhibits cytokine production
firstLine.monitoring: Blood glucose, blood pressure, bone density, weight, ophthalmologic exam
firstLine.adverseEffects: Weight gain, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, cataracts, hypertension, immunosuppression, mood changes
firstLine.evidence: Response rate 50-70%; slower onset than IVIG; cheaper; may cause steroid dependence

firstLine.dose: 5-7 exchanges over 10-14 days; maintenance: 1-2 exchanges per month
firstLine.mechanism: Removes pathogenic autoantibodies, cytokines, and other inflammatory mediators
firstLine.monitoring: Coagulation profile, fibrinogen, calcium (citrate-related hypocalcemia), hemoglobin
firstLine.adverseEffects: Hypotension, hypocalcemia, coagulopathy, infection risk from central line, hypogammaglobulinemia
firstLine.evidence: Response rate comparable to IVIG; used in severe or refractory cases; steroid-sparing

secondLine.dose: 2-3 mg/kg/day; test TPMT level before initiation
secondLine.monitoring: CBC and LFTs every 2 weeks for 2 months, then quarterly
secondLine.adverseEffects: Hepatotoxicity, bone marrow suppression, GI upset, pancreatitis, lymphoma risk

secondLine.monitoring: CBC and LFTs monthly initially, then every 3 months
secondLine.adverseEffects: GI upset, bone marrow suppression, infection, teratogenicity

secondLine.dose: IV pulse therapy 500-1000 mg/m2 monthly for 6 months or oral 2 mg/kg/day
secondLine.adverseEffects: Bone marrow suppression, hemorrhagic cystitis, gonadotoxicity, malignancy risk

secondLine.dose: 1000 mg IV day 1 and 15, then 1000 mg every 6 months
secondLine.indications: CIDP with IgG3 antibodies, autoimmune nodopathy, anti-MAG neuropathy
secondLine.adverseEffects: Infusion reactions, PML risk, hypogammaglobulinemia, infections

symptomaticManagement: Neuropathic pain: gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline, duloxetine
Physical therapy: gait training, balance exercises, strength training, stretching
Occupational therapy: hand function, adaptive equipment, energy conservation
Orthotics: ankle-foot orthosis for foot drop, wrist splints
Speech therapy: if bulbar weakness present
pathways: First-line: IVIG 2 g/kg or prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or PLEX 5-7 exchanges
Initiate with IVIG if: relapsing course, rapid progression, severe weakness, pure motor, or steroid contraindicated
Initiate with steroids if: mild to moderate, predominantly sensory, patient preference, cost considerations
After 4-8 weeks: if response, continue and slowly taper; if no response, switch to alternative first-line therapy
If refractory to two first-line therapies: consider second-line immunosuppressant (azathioprine, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, rituximab)
Maintenance: lowest effective dose of chosen therapy; regular assessments every 3-6 months

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Autoimmune-mediated demyelination of peripheral nerves and nerve roots driven by T-cell and B-cell responses against myelin antigens
TcellMediated: Activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells infiltrate peripheral nerves
T cells recognize myelin antigens (P0, P2, PMP22) and initiate inflammatory cascade
Macrophage recruitment leads to myelin stripping and phagocytosis
Th1 and Th17 polarized responses contribute to tissue injury
HumoralImmunity: B-cell activation and antibody production against myelin antigens
Autoantibodies against nodal and paranodal proteins (NF155, CNTN1, CASPR1) cause conduction failure
Complement activation contributes to myelin damage
Deposition of immunoglobulin and complement on myelin sheaths seen on nerve biopsy
Pathology: Onion bulb formation: concentric Schwann cell processes around axons indicating chronic remyelination
Endoneurial edema: increased fluid in endoneurial space
Macrophage-mediated demyelination: macrophages infiltrating and stripping myelin lamellae
Variable axonal loss: secondary to chronic demyelination or primary axonal injury in severe cases
Perivascular lymphocytic infiltration (T cells and macrophages) in nerve biopsies
CytokineMediated: Elevated TNF-alpha, IL-2, IL-6, IL-17, and interferon-gamma in serum and CSF
Chemokine upregulation (CCL2, CXCL10) recruits inflammatory cells to peripheral nerves
Matrix metalloproteinases contribute to blood-nerve barrier disruption
AutoimmuneNodopathyPathology: IgG4 antibodies against nodal antigens do not fix complement
Neurofascin-155 antibodies disrupt paranodal axoglial junctions
Contactin-1 and Caspr1 antibodies disrupt septate-like junctions at paranodes
These cause conduction block without classic demyelinating features on NCS

complications: Permanent motor disability: weakness, impaired gait, dependence on wheelchair
Sensory ataxia: persistent unsteadiness and fall risk
Chronic neuropathic pain: often difficult to manage
Respiratory failure: uncommon but can occur with severe disease or phrenic nerve involvement
Steroid complications: diabetes, osteoporosis, cataracts, weight gain, avascular necrosis
IVIG complications: thrombosis, renal failure, aseptic meningitis, hemolytic anemia
PLEX complications: central line infections, coagulopathy, hypocalcemia
Depression and anxiety: chronic disabling illness impact on quality of life
Contractures and joint deformities resulting from longstanding weakness and immobility
mustNotMiss: Distinguish CIDP from GBS: CIDP requires minimum 8 weeks of progression; GBS progresses maximally within 4 weeks
Distinguish CIDP from MMN: MMN is pure motor, asymmetric, no sensory involvement, anti-GM1 positive, responds to IVIG but NOT steroids (steroids can worsen MMN)
Check for monoclonal gammopathy in all CIDP patients (SPEP, immunofixation, free light chains)
Anti-neurofascin-155, anti-contactin-1, and anti-Caspr1 antibodies identify autoimmune nodopathy which responds poorly to IVIG but well to rituximab
Do not start steroids in suspected MMN - can cause significant worsening
Nerve biopsy is rarely needed but invaluable when vasculitis, amyloidosis, or sarcoidosis suspected
Relapsing CIDP may need long-term maintenance IVIG - do not stop abruptly after successful treatment
emergencyPriority: MODERATE - Not immediately life-threatening but significant disability can accrue; urgent neurology referral for suspected CIDP
diagnosticClues: Symmetric proximal AND distal weakness with sensory ataxia and areflexia
Progression over 8 weeks to 6 months (chronic course)
CSF cytoalbuminologic dissociation: elevated protein with normal cell count
NCS demonstrates demyelinating features (slow CV, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block, temporal dispersion, prolonged F-waves)
Response to immunotherapy (IVIG, steroids, or PLEX)
Nerve root or plexus hypertrophy and enhancement on MRI
Sensory ataxia out of proportion to weakness
exclusionClues: Acute onset with peak within 4 weeks suggests GBS, not CIDP
Pure motor, asymmetric, no sensory involvement suggests MMN
Upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, Babinski, hyperreflexia) suggest ALS or CNS disease
Axonal NCS without demyelinating features suggests axonal neuropathy or CIAP
Pes cavus, hammer toes, family history, childhood onset suggests CMT
Prominent autonomic failure, cardiomyopathy, nephropathy suggests amyloidosis
Mononeuritis multiplex pattern with systemic symptoms suggests vasculitis
No response to any immunotherapy trial should prompt reconsideration of diagnosis
